events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is of Nigerian-American descent, a non-smoker, with a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), and hypertension (on amlodipine). She is a retired nurse. BMI 29.4. ECOG performance status 1 at presentation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney (ICD-O site code C64.0), histology code 8310/3 (clear cell adenocarcinoma). Primary tumor: T3b (tumor extends into the right renal vein with macroscopic venous thrombus). Regional lymph nodes: N1 (single retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis, 2.4 cm). Distant metastases: M1 (bilateral pulmonary nodules, largest 1.8 cm right lower lobe). Sarcomatoid differentiation present in approximately 20% of the tumor. Fuhrman nuclear grade: Grade 4. Tumor size: 14.2 cm at greatest dimension. Summary stage: Distant (Stage IV). IMDC risk score: Intermediate risk (2 factors: hemoglobin below lower limit of normal, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than 1 year). Confirmation: Positive histology via surgical pathology from cytoreductive nephrectomy. Relevant biomarkers: PD-L1 expression by combined positive score (CPS) 12, tumor mutational burden (TMB) low (3.1 mut/Mb). No evidence of hereditary RCC syndrome (no germline VHL, FLCN, MET, SDHB/C/D mutations detected).

At age 63 years (initial presentation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer. Findings: Large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 14.2 x 11.8 x 10.3 cm arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney with areas of central necrosis and calcification. Macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (grade 2 Mayo thrombus). Right retroperitoneal lymph node measuring 2.4 x 1.9 cm at the level of the renal hilum. No adrenal involvement. Liver unremarkable. Bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.8 cm, right lower lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 0.7 cm, and two additional subcentimeter nodules bilaterally. No pleural effusion. No skeletal metastases identified. No brain metastases on dedicated MRI brain (performed separately). Impression: Right renal mass consistent with primary renal cell carcinoma, stage IV disease with regional lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary metastases.

At age 63 years (initial presentation), the patient had an MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast, which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal brain parenchyma. No midline shift or mass effect. Impression: No intracranial metastases.

At age 63 years, the patient had a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the primary right renal mass prior to surgical planning. Pathology type: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Specimen: Right renal mass core biopsy (3 cores). Diagnosis: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman grade 4, with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation. Microscopic description: The tumor is composed predominantly of nests and sheets of clear cells with abundant clear cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli (grade 4 features). In approximately 20% of the sampled tissue, there is transition to sarcomatoid morphology with spindled cells, high mitotic rate, and geographic necrosis. No rhabdoid differentiation identified. Immunohistochemistry: Tumor cells positive for CA9 (diffuse), PAX8 (positive), RCC antigen (positive), vimentin (positive in sarcomatoid component), CK8/18 (focal, predominantly in clear cell component). CD10 positive. TFE3 negative. CAIX strongly and diffusely positive. PD-L1 (22C3 antibody): CPS 12. Ki-67 proliferative index: approximately 35% in sarcomatoid component, 12% in clear cell component.

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass core biopsy (primary tumor). A comprehensive tumor genomic profiling panel (Foundation One CDx equivalent, 324-gene panel) was performed. Actionable/clinically relevant alterations: VHL p.R200* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, VAF 48%, homozygous loss with concurrent LOH chromosome 3p), PBRM1 p.Q1233* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, VAF 42%), SETD2 frameshift deletion (exon 12, p.K1340fs, VAF 31%), TP53 p.R175H (hotspot missense, pathogenic, VAF 28%), MET amplification (copy number 8.2, high-level amplification). Additional alterations: KDM5C p.D870G (missense, variant of uncertain significance, X-linked, VAF 89%), ARID1A p.R1989W (missense, VUS, VAF 22%), MTOR p.L1460P (missense, activating, VAF 19% - subclonal), BAP1 diploid (wild-type). Copy number alterations: Loss of chromosome 3p (encompassing VHL, PBRM1, SETD2, BAP1 locus), gain of chromosome 5q, loss of chromosome 14q. Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected. Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Tumor mutational burden: 3.1 mutations per megabase (low). PD-L1 (22C3): CPS 12. Germline findings: No reportable germline alterations in VHL, FLCN, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, MET, or PTEN. Note: MET amplification is of particular interest in the context of sarcomatoid differentiation and may have therapeutic relevance.

At age 63, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment at a tertiary academic medical center. Present: urologic oncology, medical oncology, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and radiation oncology. Discussion: Patient presents with synchronous metastatic ccRCC with large primary tumor (T3b, renal vein involvement), single-station retroperitoneal nodal disease, and limited bilateral pulmonary metastatic burden. Performance status ECOG 1. The sarcomatoid component (20%) and Fuhrman grade 4 histology are concerning for aggressive biology. Noted: MET amplification on NGS - discussed potential future relevance for cabozantinib sensitivity. The MDT consensus recommendation was for upfront cytoreductive nephrectomy given: (1) good performance status, (2) symptomatic primary tumor (flank pain, hematuria), (3) limited M1 disease burden, and (4) IMDC intermediate risk. Following surgical recovery, initiation of systemic therapy with ipilimumab plus nivolumab (CheckMate 214 regimen) was recommended given sarcomatoid histology (pre-specified subgroup with improved benefit from IO combination). Cancer present. No response assessment yet - pre-treatment.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent a right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (cytoreductive intent). Approach: Open (hand-assisted) via a right flank incision. Procedure details: Right kidney with tumor (14.2 cm), right renal vein with tumor thrombus (completely resected), right adrenal gland (adherent, included en bloc), and right retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (template: right paracaval, interaortocaval, right common iliac). Estimated blood loss: 850 mL. Intraoperative transfusion: 2 units packed red blood cells. Operative time: 4 hours 10 minutes. No intraoperative complications. Margins: Negative (R0 resection of primary). Tumor thrombus: Completely resected with negative renal vein margin.

At age 63 years, the patient had a surgical pathology report from the right radical nephrectomy specimen. Pathology type: ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Specimen: Right kidney, right adrenal gland, and retroperitoneal lymph nodes. Final diagnosis: (A) Right kidney: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman nuclear grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising 20% of the tumor volume. Tumor size: 14.2 x 11.6 x 10.1 cm. Pathologic T stage: pT3b (macroscopic venous extension into renal vein). Lymphovascular invasion: Present. Perirenal fat invasion: Present. Adrenal gland: No tumor involvement. Surgical margins: All margins negative (R0). (B) Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: 1 of 12 lymph nodes positive for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (the 2.4 cm right hilar node); 11/12 nodes negative. Largest positive node: 2.4 cm, with extranodal extension. Pathologic N stage: pN1. (C) Right adrenal gland: No metastatic carcinoma. Fibrous adhesion to the upper pole tumor noted. Additional IHC confirmatory stains: CA9 positive, PAX8 positive, TFE3 negative, consistent with clear cell RCC. Overall pathologic stage: pT3b pN1 M1, Stage IV.

At age 63 years (postoperative day 4), the patient developed a postoperative complication: ileus (Grade 2 per CTCAE v5.0). Managed conservatively with bowel rest, nasogastric tube decompression, and IV fluid support. Resolved by postoperative day 8. Additional postoperative finding: mild anemia (hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, Grade 1) managed with oral iron supplementation. Hospital course otherwise unremarkable. Discharged on postoperative day 10.

At age 63 years (6 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a post-surgical medical oncology assessment. Patient has recovered well from surgery. ECOG performance status improved to 1. Laboratory results: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (improving), creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73m2, expected given solitary kidney status), LDH 198 U/L (mildly elevated, upper limit of normal 190), corrected calcium 9.4 mg/dL (normal), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio 3.2. IMDC risk score reassessed: Intermediate risk (2 factors - anemia, time from diagnosis to systemic therapy <12 months). Plan: Initiate first-line systemic therapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance. Baseline imaging obtained. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed. Cancer present (residual pulmonary metastases). No response to treatment yet - initiating therapy.

At age 63 years (baseline pre-systemic therapy, 6 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast serving as restaging/baseline imaging. Cancer present. Findings: Status post right nephrectomy. No local recurrence at the nephrectomy bed. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (previously positive node resected). Pulmonary metastases: right lower lobe dominant nodule 1.8 cm (target lesion 1), right lower lobe second nodule 0.9 cm (target lesion 2), left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (target lesion 3). Two additional subcentimeter nodules (non-target). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No hepatic lesions. Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.4 cm. Impression: Metastatic disease limited to bilateral pulmonary nodules. Suitable for RECIST 1.1 response assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient received first-line immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate 214 regimen), initiated approximately 6 weeks following cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy. Intent: palliative/disease control. Rationale: IMDC intermediate risk, sarcomatoid differentiation (pre-specified subgroup with enhanced benefit from dual checkpoint inhibition in CheckMate 214), PD-L1 CPS 12. Premedications: Ondansetron, acetaminophen. Baseline thyroid function, glucose, LFTs, and CBC obtained and within acceptable limits.

At age 63 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately week 6 of systemic therapy), the patient developed immune-related colitis (irAE). Symptoms: watery diarrhea, 5-7 loose stools per day, mild lower abdominal cramping, no blood in stool. CTCAE Grade 2 diarrhea/colitis. Managed with: loperamide (initially, without response), then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow taper over 8 weeks. Ipilimumab held (permanently discontinued after Grade 2 immune-related colitis per protocol); nivolumab continued after steroid taper completed (per institutional protocol and patient preference after shared decision-making). Colonoscopy performed: diffuse pan-colitis without ulceration consistent with immune-related colitis. Infectious workup negative (C. difficile, CMV colitis excluded). Symptoms resolved to Grade 1 by week 3 of steroids. Complete resolution to Grade 0 by week 7 of prednisone taper.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months after initiating systemic therapy, after 4 cycles of combination then initiating nivolumab monotherapy maintenance), the patient had a medical oncology assessment with first restaging imaging. Cancer present. Mixed response observed. ECOG performance status 1. Patient reports fatigue (Grade 1), improved energy over the past 2 weeks since steroid taper completed. No further diarrhea. Thyroid function tests: TSH 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated), free T4 low-normal - consistent with subclinical immune-related hypothyroidism; levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. Restaging CT: mixed response per RECIST 1.1 - target lesion 1 (RLL) slightly increased to 2.1 cm (pseudoprogression vs. true progression being considered given immunotherapy context), target lesions 2 and 3 decreased (0.7 cm and 0.5 cm respectively). Sum of diameters 3.3 cm (stable/minimal change). Decision: Continue nivolumab, repeat imaging in 6 weeks to assess for pseudoprogression vs. true progression. Cancer present with mixed response.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging scan on nivolumab monotherapy after completing 4 cycles ipilimumab/nivolumab induction), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Mixed response. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.1 cm (from 1.8 cm baseline, +17% increase). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.7 cm (from 0.9 cm, -22% decrease). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.5 cm (from 0.7 cm, -29% decrease). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.3 cm (from 3.4 cm baseline, -3% change, RECIST stable disease). One of the previously non-target subcentimeter nodules has increased to 0.5 cm (remains non-target). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No adrenal or hepatic lesions. Impression: Mixed response with heterogeneous behavior of pulmonary metastases. Overall RECIST assessment: Stable disease. Clinical team to consider pseudoprogression of target lesion 1 in immunotherapy context.

At age 63 years (6-month restaging scan, confirmatory scan after mixed response), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Findings: Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.6 cm (from 1.8 cm baseline, +44% increase from baseline; from 2.1 cm prior scan, +24% increase - unambiguous progression of this lesion). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.6 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.5 cm (stable). Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.7 cm (from 3.4 cm baseline, +9% change). New finding: Two new pulmonary nodules right middle lobe measuring 0.6 cm and 0.4 cm. No new extrapulmonary disease. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy: New right paracaval lymph node measuring 1.1 cm (new since baseline). Impression: Mixed response with continued progression in target lesion 1 and new sites of disease (new pulmonary nodules, new retroperitoneal lymph node). Progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 criteria (new lesions).

At age 63 years (6 months into systemic therapy, progressive disease on nivolumab monotherapy), the patient had a medical oncology assessment for disease progression. Cancer present. Progressing. ECOG performance status 1 (unchanged). The patient has progressive disease per RECIST 1.1 on confirmatory scan at 6 months (new pulmonary nodules and new retroperitoneal lymph node). Although the predominant response pattern was mixed (some lesions regressing), the emergence of new sites of disease and progression of the dominant pulmonary metastasis confirm true progression. Plan: Discontinue nivolumab. Transition to second-line therapy with cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily (per CABOSUN rationale and MET amplification on NGS making cabozantinib particularly attractive - dual VEGFR/MET inhibitor). Discussed clinical trial options; no immediately eligible trials at this institution. Discussed METEOR trial data for cabozantinib as second-line post-IO therapy. Baseline labs prior to cabozantinib initiation: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, UA. Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL. Liver function normal. Initiate cabozantinib with close monitoring for hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, and hypertension.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months after initial systemic therapy start, 3 months after switching from combination to nivolumab monotherapy), the patient received second-line therapy with cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily. Intent: palliative/disease control. Rationale: Progressive disease on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab; MET amplification on tumor NGS (cabozantinib is a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, potential additional benefit in MET-amplified RCC); sarcomatoid differentiation (aggressive histology warranting active TKI). Patient counseled on TKI side effect profile including HFSR, diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, hepatotoxicity, and thyroid dysfunction. Provided with oral oncology teaching and wallet card.

At age 63 years (3 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed hand-foot skin reaction (HFSR, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia). CTCAE Grade 2: painful erythema, swelling, and hyperkeratosis over bilateral palms and plantar surfaces of the feet; blistering noted over the lateral border of the right foot. Interfering with activities of daily living (difficulty walking prolonged distances). Management: Cabozantinib dose reduced to 40 mg orally once daily per institutional guidelines for Grade 2 HFSR. Urea 40% cream applied twice daily to hyperkeratotic areas. Gel cushioned shoe inserts provided. Referral to dermatology for wound care of blistered area. Patient counseled to avoid tight-fitting footwear and prolonged standing. After 2 weeks at reduced dose: HFSR improved to Grade 1 (persistent hyperkeratosis but non-painful, not limiting function).

At age 63 years (5 weeks after initiating cabozantinib, concurrent with ongoing HFSR management), the patient developed diarrhea. CTCAE Grade 2: 4-6 loose stools per day over baseline, mild abdominal cramping, no blood in stool. Infectious workup negative. Not colitis on clinical assessment (no tenesmus, no urgency, no fever). Consistent with TKI-related diarrhea. Management: Loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (up to 8 mg/day), low-residue diet, adequate hydration encouraged. Patient maintained on dose-reduced cabozantinib 40 mg. Diarrhea improved to Grade 1 (2-3 loose stools above baseline) within 10 days of initiation of loperamide and dietary modifications. Ongoing management with as-needed loperamide.

At age 63 years (8 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient was found to have new-onset hypertension at routine clinic visit. Blood pressure 158/96 mmHg (repeated measurement confirmed). Prior blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine. CTCAE Grade 2 hypertension. Amlodipine dose increased from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. Blood pressure rechecked at 2 weeks: 138/84 mmHg (improved to Grade 1). Additional antihypertensive not required. Patient instructed on home blood pressure monitoring twice daily.

At age 63 years (3 months after initiating cabozantinib, first restaging on cabozantinib), the patient had a medical oncology assessment with restaging imaging. Cancer present. Stable disease. ECOG performance status 1. Patient tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Ongoing Grade 1 HFSR (hyperkeratosis, non-painful, managed with urea cream), Grade 1 diarrhea (1-2 loose stools above baseline, managed with as-needed loperamide), blood pressure controlled (136/82 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg). No fatigue above Grade 1. TSH 4.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 50 mcg (euthyroid). No hepatotoxicity (LFTs within normal limits). CBC: mild anemia (Hgb 10.2 g/dL, Grade 1), no thrombocytopenia. Restaging CT: stable disease per RECIST 1.1 (sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 12% from cabozantinib baseline). Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg. Repeat imaging in 3 months. Maintain current dose given tolerability. Cancer present, stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 63 years (3-month restaging scan on cabozantinib 40 mg daily), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease. Pulmonary metastases: Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.3 cm (from 2.6 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -12% decrease). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.5 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.4 cm (stable). New pulmonary nodules (RML, previously 0.6 and 0.4 cm): 0.5 cm and 0.3 cm (both slightly decreased or stable). Right paracaval lymph node: 0.7 cm (from 1.1 cm at prior scan, decreased). No new pulmonary nodules. No new sites of metastatic disease. No adrenal, hepatic, or osseous metastases. No pleural effusion. Sum of target lesion diameters: 3.2 cm (from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -14% change). Impression: Partial response approaching per RECIST 1.1 (not yet meeting -30% threshold). Overall stable disease with minor response in all target and non-target lesions. No progression.

At age 63 years (6-month restaging scan on cabozantinib, cumulative ~15 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease maintained. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.1 cm (continued slow decrease). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.4 cm. Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.4 cm. Right paracaval lymph node: 0.6 cm (near-complete response of this node). Previously noted right middle lobe nodules: 0.4 cm and 0.3 cm (both stable/minimally decreased). No new sites of disease. No pleural effusion. No osseous, hepatic, or adrenal metastases. No brain metastases on dedicated MRI brain performed concurrently. Sum of target lesion diameters: 2.9 cm (from 3.4 cm original baseline, -15% overall; -22% from cabozantinib initiation baseline of 3.7 cm). Impression: Stable disease with continued partial response to cabozantinib. No evidence of new or progressive disease.

At age 63 years (6 months on cabozantinib, approximately 15 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. ECOG performance status 1 (maintained). Patient continues on cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily. Side effect profile remains manageable: Grade 1 HFSR (minimal hyperkeratosis, asymptomatic, well-managed with emollients), intermittent Grade 1 diarrhea controlled with as-needed loperamide, blood pressure controlled (134/80 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg). Patient reports improved energy and is able to perform most activities of daily living independently. Returns to light gardening. No significant fatigue. Endocrine: TSH 3.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg (dose adjusted upward at 4-month mark). Creatinine stable at 1.4 mg/dL. No proteinuria on spot urine. Restaging CT: stable disease with continued gradual tumor reduction in all target lesions. Discussion: Patient is deriving ongoing benefit from cabozantinib. No trial eligibility at present. Continue current therapy. Discussed MET amplification and potential eligibility for future trials. Next imaging in 3 months. Cancer present, stable disease.

At age 64 years (approximately 18 months from original diagnosis, 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an interval medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. Patient continues on cabozantinib 40 mg daily with maintained stable disease over successive imaging cycles. A new issue arose: patient developed low back pain (Grade 1-2) over the past 4 weeks. Examination: no focal neurological deficits. MRI lumbar spine ordered to evaluate for potential osseous metastases versus degenerative disease (patient has history of mild lumbar spondylosis per prior incidental imaging). Laboratory review: CBC shows Hgb 10.4 g/dL (stable mild anemia), ANC 2.8 x10^9/L (normal), platelets 198 x10^9/L. LFTs normal. Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL. No dose changes to cabozantinib at this time. ECOG performance status 1. Cancer present, stable. Next restaging CT scheduled in 6 weeks.

At age 64 years (prompted by new back pain, 9 months on cabozantinib), the patient had an MRI lumbar spine with and without gadolinium. Findings: Multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease, most pronounced at L4-L5 (moderate disc height loss, facet arthropathy). No evidence of vertebral metastatic disease. No pathological compression fractures. No epidural extension or spinal cord compression. Mild foraminal narrowing at L4-L5 bilaterally from hypertrophic facet changes and disc bulge. No lytic or sclerotic vertebral lesions. Impression: Back pain most consistent with degenerative lumbar disc disease. No osseous metastatic disease.

At age 64 years (9-month restaging scan on cabozantinib, cumulative ~18 months from original diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. Cancer present. Stable disease maintained. Target lesion 1 (RLL dominant): 2.0 cm (continued gradual decrease, very slow). Target lesion 2 (RLL): 0.4 cm (stable). Target lesion 3 (LLL): 0.3 cm (minimal decrease). Right paracaval lymph node: no longer measurable (near-complete response). RML nodules: 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm (continued decrease or stable). No new pulmonary nodules. No new sites of disease. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases. Sum of target lesion diameters: 2.7 cm (from 3.7 cm at cabozantinib baseline, -27% change; approaching but not meeting PR threshold of -30%). Impression: Stable disease with continued slow response to cabozantinib. Near-complete response of the retroperitoneal lymph node metastasis. No evidence of progressive disease.

At age 64 years (9-month cabozantinib assessment, 18 months from diagnosis), the patient had a comprehensive medical oncology assessment. Cancer present. Stable disease. Patient doing well overall on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Back pain has improved substantially with physical therapy and an NSAID (naproxen, used cautiously given renal function). MRI spine: no osseous metastases (degenerative disease only). ECOG performance status 1. Side effects: Grade 1 HFSR only (prior Grade 2 fully resolved to Grade 1 with dose reduction and supportive care), intermittent very mild diarrhea (managed with PRN loperamide), blood pressure well-controlled. Quality of life assessment (FKSI-DRS): scores improved from prior assessment. Patient expresses satisfaction with current treatment. Creatinine 1.45 mg/dL (stable). No proteinuria. No thyroid dysfunction (TSH 3.5 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg). CBC: Hgb 10.6 g/dL (mild chronic anemia, stable). Discussion: Patient has durable stable disease approaching 10 months on cabozantinib with near-complete retroperitoneal lymph node response and continued slow but meaningful reduction in pulmonary target lesions. Continue current cabozantinib 40 mg. Reassess dose escalation back to 60 mg when Grade 1 HFSR fully resolves (patient defers dose escalation at this time). Next imaging in 3 months. Bone-modifying agent not indicated at this time (no osseous metastases). Cancer present, stable disease, patient tolerating therapy well.

scenario_blurb: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma stage IV with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%) in a 63-year-old, presenting with large right renal mass, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and pulmonary metastases. Undergoes cytoreductive nephrectomy. First-line ipilimumab/nivolumab with mixed response. Progression at 9 months, switched to cabozantinib with stable disease. Manages TKI-related hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea.
